text:
HEMATOLOGIC MALIGNANCY NEXT-GENERATION SEQUENCING PANEL REPORT

--- SPECIMEN AND TEST INFORMATION ---

Patient Name: Margaret Okonkwo
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Female
Ordering Physician: Hematology/Oncology Service
Institution: [Institution on file]

Specimen Type: Bone marrow aspirate (diagnostic)
Specimen Source: Bilateral posterior iliac crest aspirate
Specimen Adequacy: Adequate; 85% blasts by morphology at time of aspiration; submitted fresh in EDTA
Nucleic Acid Quality: DNA yield 2,140 ng; RNA yield 890 ng; OD 260/280 ratio 1.88 (acceptable); DIN 8.2 (high quality)
Tumor Fraction Estimate: 82% (consistent with high circulating blast burden and marrow infiltration)
Panel: Comprehensive Hematologic Malignancy Panel — 500 genes (DNA + RNA hybrid capture, Illumina NovaSeq platform)
Mean Coverage Depth: 1,247x (DNA); 312x (RNA)
Sensitivity for SNV/Indel Detection: VAF ≥ 2% (high confidence ≥ 5%)
Fusion Detection: RNA-based; sensitivity for expressed fusions ≥ 1% allele frequency
Copy Number Analysis: Performed via normalized depth-of-coverage; resolution ~3 Mb
Microsatellite Instability (MSI): Assessed across 1,189 microsatellite loci
Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB): Calculated from somatic nonsynonymous SNVs and indels across 1.1 Mb of coding sequence

--- CLINICAL INDICATION ---

Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), newly diagnosed. Patient is a 48-year-old female with a known antecedent history of myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS, RCMD subtype) diagnosed 18 months prior, managed with observation. Now presenting with leukocytosis (WBC 84,000/uL), severe anemia (Hgb 7.1 g/dL), severe thrombocytopenia (platelets 28,000/uL), gingival bleeding, and fatigue. Bone marrow biopsy confirms 62% CD34+ myeloblasts with immunophenotype consistent with AML (CD33+, CD117+, CD13+, HLA-DR+, CD34+, CD123+, CD7 partial). Auer rods identified on peripheral smear. Karyotype: 46,XX [18/20] with 2 metaphases showing del(7q). FLT3-ITD positive by FISH/PCR at high allelic ratio. AML-MRC per 2022 WHO classification. NGS panel ordered to comprehensively characterize somatic mutation landscape, confirm actionable alterations, establish MRD tracking targets, and guide risk stratification and treatment selection.

--- SUMMARY OF FINDINGS ---

PATHOGENIC / LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANTS DETECTED: 5 variants
VARIANTS OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE: 0
COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS DETECTED: 1 (del(7q), subclonal)
FUSIONS DETECTED: None
MSI STATUS: Stable (MSS)
TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN: 4.2 mutations/Mb (LOW, <10 mutations/Mb)
ELN 2022 RISK CLASSIFICATION: ADVERSE

--- ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS ---

1. FLT3 — Internal Tandem Duplication (ITD)
   Gene: FLT3 (FMS-like tyrosine kinase 3)
   Alteration Type: Internal tandem duplication (ITD), exon 14-15 junction
   Insert Length: 39 base pairs (in-frame)
   Allelic Ratio (AR): 0.82 (VAF 82%)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — HIGH ALLELIC RATIO
   Interpretation: FLT3-ITD mutations result in constitutive activation of the FLT3 receptor tyrosine kinase, driving autonomous AML blast proliferation. The internal tandem duplication involves the juxtamembrane domain, the most clinically significant location. An allelic ratio ≥ 0.5 is classified as high allelic ratio per ELN 2022 criteria, conferring substantially worse prognosis compared to low AR FLT3-ITD (median OS in large prospective cohorts < 12 months without transplant). The exceptionally high AR of 0.82 observed here indicates near-complete dominance by the FLT3-ITD-bearing clone, likely reflecting loss of heterozygosity at the FLT3 locus or acquisition of the ITD in the dominant founding clone. High allelic ratio FLT3-ITD is an independent predictor of induction failure, early relapse, and inferior OS. However, it also confers sensitivity to FLT3 tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs).
   Therapeutic Implications: DIRECTLY ACTIONABLE
   - Midostaurin (Rydapt): FDA-approved first-generation multi-targeted TKI (inhibits FLT3, KIT, PDGFR, PKC); addition to 7+3 induction and consolidation chemotherapy demonstrated significant improvement in OS in the RATIFY trial (Stone et al., NEJM 2017). Standard of care for FLT3-mutant AML induction.
   - Gilteritinib (Xospata): FDA-approved second-generation FLT3 inhibitor with superior selectivity and potency; FDA-approved for relapsed/refractory FLT3-mutant AML (ADMIRAL trial); also under investigation in frontline settings.
   - Quizartinib (Vanflyta): Second-generation Type II FLT3 inhibitor; FDA-approved in combination with 7+3 induction (QuANTUM-First trial, 2023).
   - Allogeneic stem cell transplant (alloHSCT) in first complete remission is strongly recommended given high allelic ratio and co-occurring adverse-risk mutations (see below).
   Associated Clinical Trial Eligibility: Multiple frontline and post-remission FLT3-targeted trials available; refer to institutional investigator.

--- ADDITIONAL SOMATIC MUTATIONS ---

2. DNMT3A — p.R882H (c.2645G>A)
   Gene: DNMT3A (DNA methyltransferase 3 alpha)
   Exon: 23
   Alteration Type: Missense substitution
   VAF: 44%
   Allele: Heterozygous
   Classification: PATHOGENIC
   Interpretation: DNMT3A p.R882H is a dominant-negative hotspot mutation at arginine 882 that impairs the catalytic activity of DNMT3A, a de novo DNA methyltransferase critical for establishing stem cell epigenetic patterns. This mutation results in global DNA hypomethylation and aberrant transcriptional programs. At VAF 44% (consistent with one allele in a near-diploid tumor), this mutation is inferred to represent an early or clonal event, present prior to FLT3-ITD acquisition, and likely arising in the antecedent MDS phase. DNMT3A R882H is strongly associated with adverse outcomes in AML, particularly in the context of co-occurring FLT3-ITD and NPM1 mutations; in the absence of NPM1 (as in this patient), the combination of DNMT3A R882H and FLT3-ITD high AR confers among the worst prognostic profiles in AML. Additionally, DNMT3A mutations contribute to clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) and have implications for disease relapse from persistent DNMT3A-mutant residual stem cells even after achieving morphologic remission. VAF of 44% is consistent with heterozygous mutation in a highly infiltrated marrow.
   MRD Tracking Utility: HIGH — digital PCR tracking recommended at post-induction and post-transplant timepoints.

3. ASXL1 — p.G646Wfs*12 (c.1934dupG)
   Gene: ASXL1 (additional sex combs like transcriptional regulator 1)
   Exon: 12
   Alteration Type: Frameshift insertion (1-bp duplication leading to premature stop codon at position 657)
   VAF: 41%
   Allele: Heterozygous
   Classification: PATHOGENIC
   Interpretation: ASXL1 frameshift mutations resulting in loss-of-function are among the most clinically important adverse-risk mutations in myeloid malignancies. ASXL1 is a chromatin-remodeling gene that interacts with polycomb repressive complex 2 (PRC2) and regulates histone H3K27 trimethylation. Loss of ASXL1 function leads to derepression of HOX genes and other developmental regulators, promoting myeloid transformation. The p.G646Wfs*12 frameshift at the common hotspot in exon 12 results in a truncated, likely nonfunctional protein. In the ELN 2022 framework, ASXL1 mutations contribute to the MDS-related AML/adverse-risk classification. Co-occurrence with DNMT3A, FLT3-ITD, SRSF2, and RUNX1 mutations as observed in this patient represents a high-complexity, epigenetically dysregulated clonal architecture indicative of deeply adverse biology. ASXL1 mutations are also characteristic of CH (clonal hematopoiesis) and antecedent MDS, consistent with this patient's 18-month MDS history.
   MRD Tracking Utility: MODERATE — frameshift provides highly specific digital PCR assay target.

4. SRSF2 — p.P95H (c.284C>A)
   Gene: SRSF2 (serine and arginine-rich splicing factor 2)
   Exon: 1
   Alteration Type: Missense substitution
   VAF: 38%
   Allele: Heterozygous
   Classification: PATHOGENIC
   Interpretation: SRSF2 p.P95H is one of the canonical hotspot mutations in SRSF2 (alongside P95L and P95R) that alters the RNA-binding specificity of this splicing factor, leading to aberrant mRNA processing of key hematopoietic genes. SRSF2 mutations are strongly associated with prior MDS and are a defining feature of MDS-related AML. Under the 2022 WHO classification of hematopoietic tumors, SRSF2 mutations (along with SF3B1, U2AF1, ASXL1, EZH2, STAG2, etc.) are diagnostic criteria for AML with myelodysplasia-related gene mutations, supporting the AML-MRC diagnosis in this patient. In the ELN 2022 framework, SRSF2 mutations are associated with adverse risk. Co-mutation with ASXL1 is a particularly well-characterized adverse combination in both MDS and AML, frequently arising from a shared CH/MDS ancestral clone.
   MRD Tracking Utility: MODERATE.

5. RUNX1 — p.R204Q (c.611G>A)
   Gene: RUNX1 (runt-related transcription factor 1; also known as AML1)
   Exon: 5
   Alteration Type: Missense substitution (within RUNT domain, DNA-binding interface)
   VAF: 35%
   Allele: Heterozygous
   Classification: PATHOGENIC
   Interpretation: RUNX1 encodes the RUNX1/AML1 transcription factor, a master regulator of definitive hematopoiesis. The p.R204Q substitution occurs within the RUNT homology domain at a residue critical for DNA binding and CBFbeta interaction. Loss-of-function RUNX1 mutations impair differentiation of myeloid progenitors and cooperate with FLT3-ITD to drive AML. RUNX1 mutations are classified as AML-MRC-defining mutations under the 2022 WHO classification and confer adverse prognosis per ELN 2022, particularly in the absence of the favorable CBF (core binding factor) fusion partners. The slightly lower VAF (35%) compared to ASXL1 and DNMT3A may suggest RUNX1 mutation was acquired in a subclone or secondary event during MDS-to-AML transformation, though clonal architecture analysis is not definitive from VAF alone. RUNX1 mutations are associated with poor chemotherapy response and inferior OS in AML and are a recognized driver of myeloid transformation from antecedent MDS.
   MRD Tracking Utility: MODERATE.

--- GENES EVALUATED — WILD-TYPE OR NOT DETECTED ---

The following genes were analyzed and no pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants were identified. Variants of uncertain significance (VUS) with insufficient functional evidence are excluded from this report per institutional classification policy:

Epigenetic / Methylation: TET2 — wild-type; IDH1 — wild-type; IDH2 — wild-type; EZH2 — wild-type; KDM6A — wild-type
Splicing: SF3B1 — wild-type; U2AF1 — wild-type; ZRSR2 — wild-type
Chromatin / Cohesion: STAG2 — wild-type; RAD21 — wild-type; SMC1A — wild-type; SMC3 — wild-type
Transcription Factors / Lineage: NPM1 — wild-type; CEBPA — wild-type; WT1 — wild-type; ERG — wild-type; GATA2 — wild-type; PHF6 — wild-type
Signaling / RAS pathway: NRAS — wild-type; KRAS — wild-type; PTPN11 — wild-type; CBL — wild-type; NF1 — wild-type; KIT — wild-type; JAK2 — wild-type; CSF3R — wild-type
Tumor Suppressor / DNA Repair: TP53 — wild-type; BRCA1 — wild-type; BRCA2 — wild-type; ATM — wild-type; CHEK2 — wild-type
Transcriptional Repressors: BCOR — wild-type; BCORL1 — wild-type; CUX1 — wild-type
Other AML-associated: MLL (KMT2A) — no ITD or PTD detected; MECOM (EVI1) — wild-type; DEK — wild-type; NUP98 — wild-type

--- FUSION GENE ANALYSIS (RNA-BASED) ---

Fusions Evaluated: AML-associated gene fusions including RUNX1-RUNX1T1 (t(8;21)), CBFB-MYH11 (inv(16)/t(16;16)), PML-RARA (t(15;17)), KMT2A (MLL) rearrangements (partnered with AFF1, MLLT3, MLLT10, ELL, MLLT1, AFDN, and 10 additional partners), NUP98 rearrangements, DEK-NUP214, RBM15-MKL1, BCR-ABL1, FLT3 fusions, and 46 additional hematologic malignancy-associated fusion pairs.
Result: NO FUSIONS DETECTED
Note: FLT3-ITD confirmed as intragenic tandem duplication at exon 14-15 junction; this is not a gene fusion and is correctly identified as an ITD by both DNA sequencing and RNA sequencing.

--- COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS ---

Region: Chromosome 7q — del(7q)
Type: Partial deletion, chromosome 7 long arm
Clone Size: Low-level (estimated 18-22% of cells), consistent with minor/subclonal population
Boundaries: 7q22.1 – 7q36.3 (approximate; includes regions encompassing CUX1 at 7q22, EZH2 at 7q36.1)
Pathogenic Significance: Del(7q) is a recognized recurrent cytogenetic abnormality in myeloid malignancies, particularly associated with MDS and therapy-related myeloid neoplasms. In this patient, the subclonal del(7q) (detected in 2/20 metaphases by conventional karyotype and confirmed by NGS CNV analysis) is consistent with a residual MDS-related clonal population. Under ELN 2022, del(7q) is categorized as adverse risk; however, the subclonal nature here and absence of monosomy 7 or complex karyotype somewhat attenuates but does not eliminate the adverse prognostic contribution. Notable genes within the deleted region include CUX1 (haploinsufficiency associated with myeloid transformation) and EZH2 (though EZH2 sequencing shows no point mutation in this case).
Other copy number alterations: No other arm-level or focal amplifications/deletions detected above analytical threshold.

--- MICROSATELLITE INSTABILITY (MSI) ---

MSI Status: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
Loci Analyzed: 1,189 microsatellite sites across the coding and non-coding genome
Interpretation: No evidence of deficient mismatch repair (dMMR). MSI-High or dMMR is extremely rare in de novo AML; this finding is expected and does not alter management.

--- TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB) ---

TMB: 4.2 somatic nonsynonymous mutations / Mb (LOW)
Threshold Reference: Low < 10 mutations/Mb; High ≥ 10 mutations/Mb
Interpretation: TMB is low, as expected for AML, which characteristically has a low somatic mutational burden compared to solid tumors. Low TMB in AML does not predict response to immune checkpoint blockade therapy and does not independently influence AML-specific treatment decisions. This result is consistent with a myeloid malignancy arising from clonal hematopoiesis/MDS evolution rather than a mutagenesis-driven (e.g., alkylating agent-related, radiation-related, or microsatellite-unstable) process.

--- GERMLINE DISCLAIMER ---

This assay is performed on tumor-only material (bone marrow aspirate) and is optimized for somatic variant detection. Pathogenic variants identified in genes associated with hereditary hematopoietic and cancer predisposition syndromes (e.g., RUNX1, GATA2, ANKRD26, DDX41, CEBPA) may reflect either somatic or germline alterations. The RUNX1 p.R204Q variant identified in this patient (VAF 35%) has a VAF consistent with a heterozygous germline or somatic variant; in the context of antecedent MDS and AML-MRC, a somatic origin is strongly favored clinically. However, given the association of germline RUNX1 mutations with familial platelet disorder with predisposition to AML (FPD/AML), germline confirmation via a non-tumor tissue sample (e.g., skin fibroblast or saliva) is recommended to exclude hereditary RUNX1 syndrome, particularly given potential implications for family members.

--- RISK STRATIFICATION ---

ELN 2022 Risk Classification: ADVERSE RISK

Rationale:
- FLT3-ITD with HIGH allelic ratio (AR 0.82): Per ELN 2022, FLT3-ITD with AR ≥ 0.5 in the absence of NPM1 mutation is classified as adverse risk. This patient lacks NPM1 mutation, precluding reclassification to intermediate risk.
- DNMT3A p.R882H: Mutation in a recognized adverse-risk gene per ELN 2022 criteria; co-mutation with high-AR FLT3-ITD confers particularly poor prognosis.
- ASXL1 p.G646Wfs*12: Frameshift mutation in ASXL1, an ELN 2022 adverse-risk gene.
- SRSF2 p.P95H: Splicing factor mutation associated with AML-MRC; contributes to adverse risk designation.
- RUNX1 p.R204Q: Transcription factor mutation in ELN 2022 adverse-risk category.
- del(7q): Cytogenetic abnormality associated with adverse risk (subclonal).
- AML-MRC classification: Per 2022 WHO, AML with myelodysplasia-related gene mutations and/or cytogenetic changes; inherently adverse biology.
- NPM1, CEBPA biallelic, CBF fusions: All negative — no favorable cytogenetic or molecular features present to counterbalance adverse markers.

Prognostic Summary: This patient has a highly adverse molecular landscape with 5 co-occurring pathogenic mutations spanning FLT3 (driver, actionable), DNMT3A (epigenetic regulator, early clonal), ASXL1 and SRSF2 (MDS-related chromatin/splicing), and RUNX1 (transcription factor/AML-MRC-defining), along with subclonal del(7q). This mutation cluster is representative of AML arising from a pre-existing MDS clone with superimposed FLT3-ITD acquisition as the likely leukemic transformation event. Historical data indicate that patients with this profile who do not proceed to allogeneic stem cell transplant in CR1 have estimated 3-year OS < 15-20%. Allogeneic HSCT in CR1 represents the only treatment approach with curative potential in this setting, and is strongly supported by current NCCN, ELN, and ASH guidelines.

--- MRD TRACKING TARGETS ---

The following mutations are recommended for serial MRD monitoring by digital PCR (ddPCR) or error-corrected deep sequencing post-treatment:

Primary MRD Target (highest sensitivity, most specific):
  1. FLT3-ITD (VAF 82%, insert length 39 bp) — PCR-based quantification; sensitivity to < 0.01%
  2. DNMT3A p.R882H — ddPCR; sensitivity to < 0.01%

Secondary MRD Targets (confirmatory):
  3. ASXL1 p.G646Wfs*12 — ddPCR or deep sequencing
  4. RUNX1 p.R204Q — ddPCR

Note: DNMT3A and ASXL1 mutations may persist in non-leukemic hematopoietic stem cells as clonal hematopoiesis even after AML eradication; caution is advised when interpreting persistence of DNMT3A/ASXL1 mutations at low VAF post-therapy, which does not necessarily indicate AML MRD. FLT3-ITD and RUNX1 mutations are preferred as primary markers of leukemic MRD. Multi-parameter flow cytometry (MPFC) MRD assessment at 8-color sensitivity (threshold 0.01%) is recommended in parallel with molecular MRD.

--- GENES NOT COVERED OR BELOW ANALYTICAL THRESHOLD ---

The following regions had suboptimal coverage and results should be interpreted with caution: None. All targeted regions achieved minimum coverage criteria (>200x).

--- METHODOLOGY ---

DNA and RNA were simultaneously extracted from bone marrow aspirate submitted in EDTA. Hybrid capture-based library preparation targeting 500 genes relevant to hematologic malignancies was performed. Sequencing was performed on the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform (2x150 bp paired-end reads). Bioinformatics analysis employed a validated clinical-grade pipeline with GATK HaplotypeCaller for SNV/indel calling, FACETS for copy number analysis, STAR-Fusion and ARRIBA for RNA fusion detection, and an in-house FLT3-ITD caller optimized for allelic ratio quantification. All reported variants were confirmed by orthogonal review and meet reportability criteria (minimum VAF 5% for confident calls; VAF 2-5% flagged as low-level; this report contains no low-level calls). Reference genome: GRCh38/hg38.

--- REPORT ATTESTATION ---

This report was reviewed and issued by the Molecular Pathology Laboratory. Results should be interpreted in the clinical context by the treating hematologist/oncologist. This report reflects analysis of the submitted specimen only and does not constitute a comprehensive genomic evaluation beyond the targeted panel described above. Germline pathogenic variants cannot be excluded without germline tissue analysis (see germline disclaimer above).

Molecular Pathology Laboratory Director: [Name on file], MD, PhD, FCAP
Laboratory: [Institution] Molecular Diagnostics Laboratory
CAP Accreditation: [Accreditation number on file]
CLIA Certificate: [CLIA number on file]
